narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we've carefully reviewed your application through our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and background factors. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, a Year 3 decile of 1, middle family income, an LSAT score of 23.5, an undergraduate GPA of 2.6, and plans for full-time enrollment—details that paint a picture of your unique journey so far. The model estimates a 72.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is why your application for admission was rejected; the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass to ensure strong outcomes for everyone. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which is notably lower than typical students' standings and signaled potential challenges in sustaining academic momentum. Your LSAT score was the next biggest driver toward failure, as it falls well below the average that correlates with bar success for applicants like you. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA worked in your favor, pulling the prediction slightly toward passing since it's closer to the norm despite not being top-tier. Being a full-time student also helped a bit, aligning with the majority of successful candidates and nudging things toward a pass. Your Year 1 decile and family income had minimal sway, neither strongly helping nor hurting the outcome. Compared to typical students—who often enter with higher deciles around 5-6, LSATs near 37, and GPAs over 3.2—your profile stands out as riskier in those key academic areas that historically predict bar passage. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman determined to break barriers in law, but rather a data-driven call to build a stronger foundation. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying once you've addressed these gaps—many students in similar situations have succeeded by doing just that. We're rooting for you and encourage you to reach out for resources tailored to underrepresented applicants like yourself. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
